skill.name: documentation-writer
skill.description: Technical documentation writer for the Logseq Template Graph project. It analyzes project files and templates, then drafts READMEs, user guides, technical references, and API documentation in the project style.
skill.summary: Create clear Logseq Template Graph documentation from existing code, templates, and project conventions.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows they are Markdown templates, example code fences, relative links, and prose. No malicious intent, prompt injection, data exfiltration, executable payload, or hidden network behavior was found. The remaining risk is low because the skill legitimately guides agents to edit documentation files and test commands.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown Templates
low_findings.description: The shell and backtick findings appear in Markdown examples, code fences, and inline path formatting. I did not find executable Ruby or shell code in the skill file, so these are false positives for direct command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation templates and fenced examples, not runnable code. The file contains no script body that executes these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Network And Weak Crypto Indicators Are Benign Text
low_findings.description: The detected URL is a schema.org reference used in example documentation. The weak cryptography detections occur in prose or Markdown structure, and I found no cryptographic implementation or network request behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only confirmed URL is a public reference link inside an output example. No code imports HTTP libraries or implements weak cryptographic functions.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Finding Is A Relative Documentation Link
low_findings.description: The path traversal pattern is a recommendation to use relative Markdown links. It does not read files through user-controlled paths or perform filesystem traversal at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is a Markdown link example, not filesystem access code. There is no runtime path handling around this location.

low_findings.title: Documentation Workflow Uses File Edits And Command Testing
low_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to read, write, and edit documentation files, and to use Bash for command validation. This is expected for documentation work but creates a minor operational risk if applied to untrusted repositories or destructive commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly lists Read, Write, Edit, and Bash as tools for the workflow. Risk is limited because the guidance is user-directed and does not embed hidden commands.

content.value_statement: Project documentation often becomes incomplete or inconsistent as code changes. This skill turns existing files and templates into clear READMEs, guides, references, and API docs.
actual_capabilities: Drafts module READMEs with overviews, classes, properties, usage examples, and Schema.org references.
Creates task-focused user guides with prerequisites, steps, examples, troubleshooting, and next steps.
Builds technical references that describe components, locations, dependencies, interfaces, and integration notes.
Documents scripts, commands, and APIs with syntax, parameters, options, outputs, examples, and exit codes.
Reviews existing project files to keep terminology, structure, links, and examples consistent.
Applies a quality checklist for accuracy, completeness, formatting, and documentation freshness.
limitations: It is tailored to the Logseq Template Graph project and may need adaptation for unrelated repositories.
It relies on accessible source files, templates, and existing documentation for accurate results.
It cannot guarantee command behavior unless commands are tested in the current environment.
It does not replace maintainer review for architecture decisions or public release wording.

use_cases.title: Document A New Module
use_cases.description: Generate a README that explains module purpose, classes, properties, examples, and related modules.

use_cases.title: Refresh User Guides
use_cases.description: Update installation or workflow guides after commands, dependencies, or setup steps change.

use_cases.title: Create Script References
use_cases.description: Turn script behavior into structured command documentation with options, outputs, and troubleshooting notes.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Module README
prompt_templates.prompt: Write documentation for the person module. Read the module files, identify classes and properties, and create a clear README with examples.

prompt_templates.title: Update A User Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the installation guide with the current setup steps. Keep the existing structure, test commands where possible, and add troubleshooting notes.

prompt_templates.title: Build An API Reference
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for scripts or commands.
prompt_templates.prompt: Document the build script as an API reference. Include purpose, syntax, parameters, options, outputs, examples, and related commands.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Documentation Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the documentation for gaps across user guides, module READMEs, and developer references. Propose updates, then draft the highest priority sections.

output_examples.input: Document a new module for managing people and contacts.
output: A README overview of the module purpose.
Schema.org reference notes and related module links.

output_examples.input: Update the installation guide after a tooling change.
output: Clear setup steps in the existing guide style.

output_examples.input: Create reference documentation for a build command.
output: Exit code and related command notes.

best_practices: Provide the source files or directories that should drive the documentation.
Ask for command validation only when the local environment can safely run the commands.
Review generated docs for project-specific terminology before publishing.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to invent module behavior without source files or examples.
Do not use it for unrelated projects without adapting the style and structure requirements.
Do not run generated commands against production data without manual review.

faq.question: What type of documentation does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates module READMEs, user guides, technical references, API references, and documentation updates.

faq.question: Is this skill specific to one project?
faq.answer: Yes. It is written for the Logseq Template Graph project and its documentation structure.

faq.question: Can it edit existing documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It can update existing guides and references when source files and project context are available.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No hidden commands were found. It recommends testing commands for accuracy when the user asks for documentation work.

faq.question: Can it generate examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes practical examples based on real project data instead of placeholders.

faq.question: What review is still needed?
faq.answer: Maintainers should verify accuracy, links, command output, and release-sensitive wording before publishing.
